title: Xiaomi-Robotics-1 | Hacker News
excerpt: I'm not sure why some comments here are so pessimistic. I was grinning ear to ear while seeing the video. You're telling me we've finally got robots that can do our laundry? And the model is at least as free as beer?
content_html:
I'm not sure why some comments here are so pessimistic. I was grinning ear to ear while seeing the video. You're telling me we've finally got robots that can do our laundry? And the model is at least as free as beer?

To quote the lyrics/meme, we used to pray for times like this. This is what robotics should be used for.


There tends to be a lot of low-key (and some not-so-low-key) anti-Chinese xenophobia on HN, so that's likely a big part of it too.


More like pro-US exceptionalism, and any announcement for products created outside of it (whether it's EU or China) is met with doubts and skepticism.

Unless something is from Japan, then it's perfect and any criticism is because you don't appreciate its unique cultural heritage.


There has not been a pro-US exceptionalism tone in any HN thread in awhile. Go to any thread recently and it's pretty anti-capitalism/anti-US.


They are likely Effective Altruists highly attuned to AGI (With current LLM's) risks, fearing an LLM uprising and human extinction, which they regard as the world's most critical challenge.


the name stopped being relevant a long time ago when they went from rating charities to coming up with horror stories about ai. "ineffective egotism" would be better for what they are now.


I've definitely noticed even more than that - any submissions painting the current USA in a good light are instantly and mercilessly buried (you can check my submissions for examples), while anything even remotely on the "USA bad" side gets to the top. It's really a sad state of affairs.


You may take this as “USA bad”, comment, but let’s take the U.S. life expectancy reaches record high (in 2024) link you posted.

That sounds like a good thing and it is. But it’s not a US story. In fact, other wealthy countries didn’t just already have a higher baseline life expectancy pre-COVID, they rebounded to it post-COVID sooner than the U.S. was able to its already lower level.

So what this actually is, is a story of “global modern medicine good”, and unfortunately, even within that, the U.S. is among the worst performing.


I tried to read the Washington Post op-ed piece you shared on reasons to be optimistic about America’s future. As soon as I scrolled down, the content was replaced by a patriotic fireworks animation and a paywall. Seemed a bit on the nose.


aka this very thread - top comment and most of the entire comment section has been taken over by literally "how dare anyone say anything even remotely negative about China, what's wrong with you".


Is that so shocking on dominantly US forum? I wouldn't expect different situation, people are not 100% objective even if they try hard.

That being said, >95% of the world lives outside US and in recent times (culminating with orange man's second term but started way before) US has alienated literally every single other country, democratic or not. This is what happens when reins are firmly held by unstable people full of pettiness and mental issues, plus visibly corrupt too.

So division deepens as seen also here, and as much as 20th century was a century of US, 21st century is and will be one of China. Every move US makes now just pushes whole situation further into that direction. So folks cheer for their own camp, not some larger common group of aligned friends.


It's strange because my impression has been pro-Chinese comments on HN. I suppose it depends on the topic, the time of day, and how many bots each government is employing on a given day for the key words and their compute budget. This submission seams rather balanced to me.


Is there evidence of any state-sponsored bots at all? It seems like this has become an accepted fact but I don’t see any data indicating it’s actually real.


Pretty sure there isn't.

HN has lots of hellbanned bots lately (I have showdead on [1]) but I never notice ones pushing a political slant. But I suppose nation states conducting a campaign on Hacker News would be much too cunning to make the bots obvious

[1] because many posts get wrongly flagged, apparently in part by trigger-happy users who will flag anything with incidental similarities to AI prose (everyone, PLEASE STOP DOING THAT), in part because HN's automoderation is severe for new users, and in part because plenty of people get hellbanned either unfairly or although they do still write plenty of worthwhile posts. I even rescued someone who it turned out had been hellbanned by a mod by accident.


Well. I already decided some time ago that I don't want to have my money go into US corporations as long as the orange King is living out his genocidal fantasies (and even without that, I am already annoyed to no ends about Apple, Microsoft, Facebook and Google abusing everyone else so blatantly and violently, including buying legislation - look at the mandatory age sniffing movement before you can access information on the www any longer; this is fascism, and children are abused as fake reason for change here). But I am not all that happy about China becoming so dominant in the whole product chain either. I think the production should be more balanced world-wide, globally. And also, very much so, locally. I am also aware that this is very hard to do, for various reasons, but still if I have a little influence then I really would rather not empower the USA or China any more than they already abuse everyone else here. And both do, by the way.


Palestinians? Israelis are loathe to admit it but that country wouldn't last a year without support- from non Jews!


I disagree with your assertion that Trump is somehow committing genocide against Palestinians while also acknowledging that the situation there is awful and I pray it ends soon.

But last I checked the Israeli-Palestinian conflict is a few thousand years older than our sitting president.


Right, but the original commenter said "living out his genocidal fantasies", which means he is actively performing genocide on someone and it also implies this is something being done with active, excitement.

I'm simply asking who are these people being genocided?


western elites are currently at odds with the chinese elites and every impressionable idiot is being inundates with antichinese propaganda.

Lets be frank, every single issue we have with the CCP is either also being done by the west or in the works.

chinese oppression of the uighurs is nothing compared to whats going on in Gaza.

china forces prisoners to peel garlic while american prisoners are paid pennies a day to fight wildfires while being prohibited from being paid for those very same skills as firefighters when rehabilitated.

america is quickly workin on legislation to to make criticism of certain foreign governments illegal with very real reprucussons (revoking passports).

At least china has a working power grid and high speed train network.


It's possible to intelligently discuss the merits of Chinese society and also acknowledge that it's a brutal autocracy.

Making some kind of equivalence of the two in terms of the rights of the individual is so ridiculous that nobody is going to take anything you say after that seriously, you do your arguments a disservice by doing it.


That you can freely talk about nspm-7 on an American forum without fear of being disappeared shows that America and China are not at the same level.

Yet.


"Yet" is the keyword. it won't be long before we arent' any different from china at the rate we are going at and I think a lot of us would be better off preventing the west from turning into an oppressive autocracy instead of judging china.


Saying that the US and China are currently the same is admitting defeat and giving up the fight before the fight has actually been lost.


You can talk about it, but the hidden social credit system operated by the 3-letter agencies in conjunction with techno-feudalism oligarchs designed explicitly to bypass constitutional rights is very real and has very real long term consequences for your privacy and freedom.


It’s not just HN. The USG has been manufacturing consent for war with China (trade or otherwise) in the US population for decades. Same goes for Iran.


Every single thing that’s come out of China in the entire AI/Robotics space lately has been a massive net positive for the world

Open Source is a good thing, regardless of where it comes from


It's also relevant that China isn't open sourcing results because that's something China will do forever on some philosophical grounds. They do it because it is currently in the best interests of the Chinese state (and the world, as it turns out, coincidentally) but as soon as that stops being true there is zero chance it'll continue to be open source.


Yeah the US needs to step up with its Open AI research here, they should start some kind of "Open AI" collective to release models free to the world, do it all open like


Is there someone saying you should hate something?

If we want open source technology we should recognize that the current arrangement is non sustainable - the moment china doesn't have an american AI industry foil to play against it'll immediately close. It's up to the US to build more open source models. We already own the content used to train them, collectively. We should own the results as well. Make the big AI companies release weights.


Eh, I take what I can get. A lawyer doesn't argue that his client is innocent (though that might be tha case), because of some innate sense of justice, but because he gets to paid so.


Yes, this is the correct attitude. Fire is useful, but don't forget it burns you when you stop paying attention.


I keeping saying this, the best AI / LLM coming out isn't another question / answer chatbot or automate my email and essay.

It is Robotics! Finally doing my dishes and laundry for pete sake! And hopefully help me to find stuff I keep forgetting where I put it.


This super interesting link showing things that were impossible even a couple of years back, sitting below the day old news about Kagi's me too browser tells everything about HN audiences. West will fall behind in robotics and mostly because of the wrong priorities of its VCs and developers.


> You're telling me we've finally got robots that can do our laundry?

No, we don't.

I wish we did, that would be amazing.

There have been demos like this over a decade. But they are just that, demos. Demos done in a controlled environment.

If there was a robot I could buy that could effectively fold laundry or do dishes, I would gladly drop 50-100k on it, but such a thing doesn't exist and likely won't for a long time.


You would honestly pay 50-100k on a robot that just folds laundry or does your dishes? In the US you can get a maid that cleans once a week for $150-250 and would do a much better job. By the time that cost would equal this robot, the robot would be obsolete, not to mention any maintenance costs.

Also dishes are the easiest chore to do around the house, especially if you clean them after use instead of letting them pile up.


As long as it was the kind I could buy spare parts for, sure. And it wasn't connected to the internet. Not having cleaning staff in the house periodically would be a huge quality of life improvement.


I honestly think I would. I have a maid come every two weeks which is of course not often enough for dishes/laundry. A maid that only came weekly couldn't stay on top of dishes either but they could do laundry fine sure. I do a lot of cooking and I find constantly doing dishes and loading/unloading the dishwasher to be a huge never-ending pain. A device that costs 100k and would free of me that sisyphean task forever would be a godsend. Also you can have the robot work anytime whereas a maid is probably on a schedule.

And not to move the goal posts, because my original post did only mention laundry and dishes, but I assume once a robot reached a point where it could do laundry/dishes effectively without supervision, that it could do a variety of other tedious household tasks. 50-100k would be well worth it IMO.


Well in that case the robot should cook as well! Sure, if the robot could do all the chores in a household that price looks more reasonable.

I quite enjoy doing laundry myself though, it's a routine I do at night and find relaxing.


Wow, some people really dislike any inconvenience in your lives, don't you?

What will you do pray tell with that extra time? I doubt it will be spent on loftier pursuits. In fact I'm quite certain that for the vast majority it won't be.

Wall-E was a documentary - just shot in the past.


Technology has already removed a huge amount of inconveniences from our lives that we take for granted, such as not having to engage in backbreaking subsistence agriculture to survive. Why do you draw the line at a robot doing dishes but not a tractor plowing a field?

By your logic, why shouldn't I get rid of my dishwasher and laundry machine?


Some things are necessary for survival and flourishing while others merely remove inconvenience from our lives.

Agricultural technology feeds the people we have around the globe (arguably we have too many people for the planet's capacity but that's a separate topic). Meanwhile some technologies just feed the hedonistic instinct.

Of course the line is blurry in places but cleaning up your messes is definitely the top end of the hedonism treadmill. I may be radical in my views here as I consider a personal automobile one such hedonistic luxury that the society would be better off without. Consider the Netherlands here. By just about any metric you care to measure it's one of the best flourishing societies in the world handily beating the US and Canada on an array of well being stats. Yet they are actively shunning cars and pushing their society towards the "inconvenience" of walking, cycling and public transport. Their society as a whole benefits in many ways.

Most of the world (with the USA leading the pack) is conveniencing itself into an early grave.


Thanks for taking the time to explain your point of view here.

I think what I'm struggling with is what criteria (or who) determines which inconveniences are acceptable to remove and which ones aren't. For example, why is a standard kitchen dishwasher acceptable but a robot that saves you 5-10 minutes a day of loading/unloading the dishwasher not? Is a regular Kenmore dishwasher acceptable, or does it push too far towards the top of the hedonism treadmill, and people should have to do dishes by hand?

> What will you do pray tell with that extra time? I doubt it will be spent on loftier pursuits. In fact I'm quite certain that for the vast majority it won't be.

I actually agree with you here. Judging by how most people spend their time, the majority will fill 10 minutes of toil saved with Youtube shorts and Instagram, but there are still a minority of people who will put that time normally spent on toil towards higher aims.

For the former category, if they were just doing mindless toil anyway, what does it really matter if they waste that time some other way?


Because doing chores is not a complete waste of time. It's a mild physical activity and at the very least some rest for the brain and they eyes from the glowing screen.

As for who decides what is hedonism and what is a necessity? I think the society does - in a soft way. When people see others adopt more discipline and shun convenient doodads for slightly more physical work the society as a whole becomes a bit more badass.

I personally would rather bike and walk everywhere. Even if it takes longer and exerts more effort. If some day the majority sees it my way the society as a whole starts to soft push in that direction by altering the city infrastructure to be friendlier to bikes and pedestrians and less accommodating to cars. That's just one example.

Doing your dishes in the sink may feel like a terrible chore if you grew up with a dishwasher. I did not. I can live without one just fine. The benefit is that you get a bit of zen time away from the screen and there is no dishwasher to break and leak every few years.


>Meanwhile some technologies just feed the hedonistic instinct.

Ah, Calvinism rears it's ugly head again. Life is suffering, God doesn't want it otherwise.


The singularity is literally here right now! Some people will never be happy! This is easily the most amazing thing I have seen in my life! These things will be taking care of and raising babies very soon!


The pessimism in the comments here is entirely the reflection of the times we're living in.

Technology got weaponized against the average citizen.

It has become a synonym for unemployment, enshittification, mass surveillance and social erosion.

If this video appeared in 60's or 70's, people would be in awe imagining the future where robots do everything.


Yes but unemployment where a small cadre of rich elites own the robots and the rest of us are serfs is very different from the public owning and benefiting from the robots in the form of strong social services and UBI.


This seems more likely than racism, or us exceptionalism as mentioned above.

People are and should be skeptical of this. The natural response would be

a) It will put me out of a job

b) I won't be able to afford it anyway.


Those of us who liked the open web and P2P and hacking on projects all the time are seeing these models as a way to rebuild the internet in a shape that pleases us. To build anything and everything we want. Sans opportunity cost.

And that's the real value - you don't have to burn the candle of your life away to get what you want. You'll have so much more time now that you aren't AST wrangling.

Opportunity cost is vanishing (or at least the cost is multiple orders of magnitude smaller). And that's the most exciting thing that's happened in tech since the internet.

I'll probably be able to make my own phone operating system.

I'll probably be able to decompile N64 games, port them to Rust, combine them, extend them. In a weekend.

I'll probably be able to make my own music management platform while simultaneously working on a few robotics projects.

A find my phone button.

Automate everything.

And that's maybe a month of projects.


The whole thing feels like that the US collectively isn't feeling confident about itself in the coming creativity-limited world that AI/AGIs supposedly bring, and that it's not happy about that pessimistic rational projections. I don't know exactly why wouldn't it be or why nothing seem to be done about it, though. Both look to be fundamentally solvable problems to me.


> You'll have so much more time now that you aren't AST wrangling.

So much time because you were fired and have nothing to do. Nothing is as good as oodles of quality time worrying about funding your family's needs. It's amazing.


Robots will take jobs from massive amount of low income people.

AI will take jobs from a massive amount of avg and high income people.

The disruption ahead is getting pillowed right now due to physical limitations of progress like amount of energy, building DC, doing research, training models (its still takes 1-3 month).

Also a massive buffer will be the boomer going into retirement.

Depending on the speed of all of this, we might get a lot poorer before we get abundance everyerwer


> Depending on the speed of all of this, we might get a lot poorer before we get abundance everyerwer

not "might", will

and it's a hell of a leap to assume massive abundance will follow -- for someone, perhaps, but not the average person


At some point it will be cheaper to give everyone bread instead of paying for your private military.

But yeah lets see what going to happen.


But there's no feeling of power with giving everyone bread. Billionaires seem to have more than just an addiction to big numbers, maybe seem to want to rule over others.


You have no evidence of this.

Also: you can literally make incredible software that would take half a year right now for $200/month. Or free if you run local open weights.

You can do branding, marketing, design. You can create comics and movies and video games.

You can make an AI tool right now and get customers today. You have no idea how wildly popular this segment is. How much people willingly engage with this stuff and want it.

Yet you choose to disengage and complain about a future that hasn't happened.

It's more likely that firms will need to hire more to stay competitive. Or that workers will fire their old jobs and break out on their own.

Something I used to think was impossible: competing with Google, Apple, Stripe. Not anymore. I think you can compete with anyone.


I saw robots doing this in tech demos 15 years ago. And even those robots weren't doing anything new. Like all AI models, this is just AI repackaging and rebranding of things that robots have already been programmed to do using more classical control methods. The idea to do everything via AI was of course thought of for a long time, but the conclusion has been we have faster and more reliable methods to do the same thing with less compute, and that's still true.

During my undergrad I worked on a project where we set off a bunch of walking robots, recorded their walks, and then used the data as the basis for a learned walking controller. I see this Xiaomi model as pretty much like that.

It's a nice technology and maybe could lead to better folding one day but unfortunately it's not "finally we have robots that can do our laundry" (yet).


Yeah exactly. I've been seeing demos like this for ages. I'm kinda shocked at the lack of skepticism in the comments here. The hackernews comment sections have changed a lot these past couple years...


> You're telling me we've finally got robots that can do our laundry?

I’m still waiting for it to peel, slice n dice onions so that I do not have to cry while doing so, and not pay $8k for that robot.


Did you see any of the MSE numbers? around 50% success rates in the tasks.

Anyone in robotics can tell you that building an 80% solution is doable, very fast. Covering the last 20% can take your entire lifespan


> You're telling me we've finally got robots that can do our laundry?

Did you watch it actually fold that shirt? Super slow and bad at it.


Is a Chinese robot vacuuming and mopping my floor every day? Yes it is. Could I do it better manually? Absolutely. I still prefer the robot to do an ok job than to do it myself.


The difference is that vacuuming seven times as often can compensate for a less effective process. Folding 7 times doesn't eliminate wrinkles.


Most people in software misunderstand how physical goods work. When making machines you don't care about getting everything perfect all the time, you care about staying within tolerable limits all the time.

How bad do the folds in your laundry need to be for them to be no different then just being dumped in a pile? As long as we are above that point the robot is useful.


I think there is a stark contrast between the plus or minus micron level tolerances, and whatever is being churned out of LLM ('not getting it perfect all the time')


I don’t need it to do it faster than me, I just need it to do it instead of me.

Plus it’s obvious it will get better.


> Super slow and bad at it

Eh, this still has value for me if it can do it reliably. Leaving a robot to badly fold my t-shirts overnight and then badly put them in my closet the next day is fine. I don't need my casual wear beautifully folded. But I do need it organised, and that's apparently a chore I'll just never learn to like doing.


Absolutely!

Also if this scale anywhere close to LLMs in 2 years they will fold at super human speed and much better than me.


The real question is whether a hardware platform today would be good enough.

The model is software on a server or in the cloud, so the real question is are there any show stoppers from the hardware for going faster?


Physics. Right now the clothes folding is computationally limited, but assuming we get faster computers to get them folding faster, gravity and air resistance are still going to take their time.


Exactly. I will pay money for a robot to take laundry out of the washing machine and hang it to dry and then fold it and put it away or just sort it by size. This is a chore that my wife and I have to do several times every week.


My solution: stuff the clothes from the washer into the dryer, then stuff them into my wardrobe. I buy clothes that don't get wrinkled much even if simply shoved in the wardrobe and if they do, I tolerate it and wear them anyway.


Ha! You remind me of something I used to tell my colleagues back when I held a regular job. They would compliment me on my highly patterned shirts (eg. A very small floral pattern repeated). I would explain that a shirt like that just requires low spin in the washer, and then drying on a hanger. Whatever wrinkles are left are almost invisible due to the pattern. With collars and cuffs I’d just literally manually stretch/tension them while they were still wet. Bada bing bada boom.


Sorry, but if you're buying clothes that have manual drying steps, it's gonna be a hard sale to the general public.


This is a mostly American perspective that I find endlessly amusing. I only have a handful of clothing items that say that tumble drying is fine on the tag, and most of my clothing is very casual wear (t-shirts, jeans, sports clothing). Dryers eat up clothing like it's nobody's business. Plus, electricity costs money while the sun is free. And while yes, the time to hang laundry has value, it takes 10 minutes to hang a load. If it rains, I hang the clothes indoors with a dehumidifier which is cheaper to run than a dryer and dries clothes in a matter of a couple of hours.


I was referring to shirts specifically. If you put any kind of cotton shirt in a dryer it’s almost certainly going to need ironing. Waiting for a shirt to dry naturally shouldn’t be a big deal.


So what? it can take hours to do it and I can focus on something else. If these robots actually become available on affordable price I would buy one without blinking. It would totally free up my weekends, no more hours wasted doing laundry, cleaning the apartment etc.


My dishwasher is also super slow and bad at it. But you know what? I still prefer that over doing it myself.


Sure but that holds true for most new tech. Expensive and slow but it can only get better or stay the same. I'm thinking it's only going to get better.


I can also vacuum my floor 3x faster than a robot vacuum. But so what, I'm not there when it happens.


> I'm not sure why some comments here are so pessimistic.

Actually, it seems like 99% of the comments are glazing this with zero skepticism actually. If anything the comments are too enthusiastic and lack the measured discernment that was more common on HN 5+ years ago.


> This is what robotics should be used for.

I disagree. We should use robotics to extend our reach into hazardous environments, enhance our physical capabilities, or to provide a steady handling of things where it's absolutely needed - doing laundry in my household is none of these.

Folding laundry takes me 10 mins every 10 days. I organize my closet once in a while according to my sense of fashion, and now I'll just need to keep that structure. I don't always like to do these chores, but I have no issue in acknowledging them as part of my responsibilities. Why would I even automate[0] that?

Robotics is costly, and I see no way how that would change anytime soon. That isn't a flaw of some kind either, it's rather just one of the limitations that needs to be accounted for, neglecting that would be detrimental to the future I envision.

Don't get me wrong, I love robotics with a passion, as engineer I like to tinker, and I'm a big admirer of FESTOs bionic research[1]. It's just that the field suffers from an "overpromised and underdelivered" aura as far as I can remember, and I remember the presentation of ASIMO[2] 26 years ago.

Some people would like to see the field hyped up, focused on the consumer like every other field in tech, probably for financial gain, but I personally see the value in its downplay. To be honest, I love these reality checks, as I see them as a valuable part of the learning process all together - a journey for what it means to automate, and what it means to define as human natural chore.

[0]: https://xkcd.com/1205/

[1]: https://www.festo.com/us/en/e/about-festo/research-and-devel...

[2]: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/ASIMO


Sure but the robots that extend our capabilities often start by replicating our current capabilities, even if those are as simple as folding clothes. It provides a baseline for the next generation of robots that can do more.


You could replace "robotics" with "AI" in what you wrote above, and it would sound like what people were saying about llms when they first came out. Not trying to glaze one way or another but historically this take is outdated.


> I'm not sure why some comments here are so pessimistic.

Time and time and time and time and time again, technologists are always naïve to never realize that the road to hell is paved with good intentions.

I am expecting the opposite effect to happen with the actual use of robotics. Mass displacement of every sector of labor.

This is not ruled out.


Mass displacement of every sector of labour. Dissent policed by Boston Dynamic's robodogs. You will own nothing and be happy.


A subset of the people on HN hate AI and yell "slop" at everything they see. It's even worse if you go to other social websites.

Just ignore these people. If they don't use AI, they likely won't remain practicing in our field for much longer. They will naturally self-select themselves out of relevancy.

I was Claude coding and got hit by the 3 AM reset bug. I went on reddit and saw hundreds of other engineers hit by the same. We are different people than the AI haters, and there has never been something so powerful it's kept me up past 3 AM every single night working beyond every fiber of my body telling me to sleep. I feel empowered as if I command an army. And there are millions of us. The sheer might of all of this will win out over negativity.

I've built multiple successful businesses in the last year. If you're doing this with any sort of interest in self-employment you don't need your employer anymore.

I have never been happier. This is the best future, and I feel lucky to be living it. It's what I dreamed about when I was a kid, and it feels magical. As far as I can tell, the future has no limits.


This is not meant as an attack or to sound cruel but you are suffering from some degree of psychosis. Please look after yourself.


It's always funny seeing people that write like this as if they are somehow exempt from the future we're seemingly rushing into. Bro, you prooompting yourself into unconsciousness is not going to make you not obsolete. Unless you're being sarcastic in which case poe's law.


Working past your personal bedtime, part of an imaginary movement of millions might, ignoring the negative aspects of AI, empowered like commanding an army. This frankly sound psychotic, and it's exactly why uncontrolled AI usage is seen so critical by many.

And all other devs will leave the field just because they prefer to work differently? That is not only not realistic, it is violence fuel.


You will have to work long nights when you try to build a business of your own while continuing to work a regular job.

Talking about psychosis and "violence fuel" while handwaving at negative aspects of AI does not seem substantial.

I would be more curious to hear details about how OP built the claimed businesses.

I will not see this info here if people who build things and are enthusiastic about AI are driven away by downvotes and negative comments.


The frustrating part is that these posts will never post any details about the business, despite the fact that it would immediately invalidate most criticism.

It seems to be a similar phenomenon to productivity "gurus" who write and evangelize polyphasic sleep and "grindset" and so on - when you ask what they're using these superpowers to actually accomplish, they're very quiet. Or it's "coming soon." Or "I'm not going to waste time explaining to someone who obviously doesn't ~believe~." It quickly becomes religious.


You're not going to be able to keep up with the market if you're not using AI.

100kloc in a day is table stakes now. And that's just where we are today - imagine a year from now.

You can't continue to be a horse cart driver and get paid for it. Or an abacus user. We're moving past it now.


Even if you're Fabrice Bellard, you can't compete with how fast these models are. They'll run circles around any human.

I don't know what else to tell you other than you should try the models again and update your assumptions.

A year ago I thought LLMs were trash. This year has been a completely eye opening experience.

Do not be so stubborn that your opinions are unshakable. Something exceptional is happening and it deserves just a little bit more of your attention.


it's fine it can run in circles all it wants, i just want a calm, stable life with purpose (no running an llm ain't it)


> there has never been something so powerful it's kept me up past 3 AM every single night working beyond every fiber of my body telling me to sleep. I feel empowered as if I command an army. And there are millions of us

Yelling "slop" at a clothes-folding robot is stupid, but reading this kind of shit makes me want to embody Ted Kaczynszky and go live in the woods.


Ahah you AI bros can't stop talking about Claude and llm, and telling stories of how great it was that one time, even when they are not the topic at hand.

> I've built multiple successful businesses in the last year. If you're doing this right you don't need your employer anymore.

Right


Rolled my eyes at "I've built multiple successful businesses in the last year". This person is clearly in some sort of manic state.


We nearly destroyed our planet to get there. Technocrats here applaude the new technological achievements that make life a little easier (less boring?) for the top one percent while willfully ignoring the associated costs for everyone else. Manufacturing these at scale will only make things worse. We are approaching peak decadence ever faster.


I am of course impressed by the model and the robot. Great stuff,

But the downside is that won't we all just turn into the obese, stupid humans at the start of the WALL-E film?

Mindfulness teaches us that we can enjoy the washing up, the laundry, planting seeds, tending the garden. It connects us to something. Without this will we end up with a rise in mental health issues, because for one thing people will just end up on social media in the time they save.

How are these models compared to world models by Waymo and Tesla, assuming their models are trained for recognising objects that can be further generalized for everyday chores?


Compared how? Architecturally? In training methods? In performance?

The remarkable thing about this modern "full VLA" approach is that it doesn't train for an explicit "world model" at all. There's no factored world state, no pool of trajectory rollouts being sampled from. Instead, everything is rolled into the same VLA stack that does all of it internally.

The world model exists, but it's implicit - the VLA learns one, but only because it helps its training objectives. Trajectories are planned ahead sometimes, also implicitly, for the same reasons.

It's a very "bitter lesson" way of doing things.

As far as I know, Tesla's stack is somewhat similar architecturally - a highly integrated end to end model - but the training process for FSD does include factored "world modeling" side objectives to force the stack to develop driving-relevant internal world representations. So it's in a middle ground between a classic "world model" system and this flavor of VLA - albeit surprisingly close to the latter.

Whether the world modeling side objectives are principled and justified or a leftover from the days of insufficient scale is unclear.

Which is reall just a patch for the problem of "On one hand… On the other hand… …On the OTHER hand…".It's worth noting that the one-other-gripping pattern seems meant to convey two considerations and end with the strongest of three.The English one-other pattern doesn't necessarily require the second consideration to dominate. They could be equal, or they might merely introduce concerns that haven't been evaluated to conclusion yet.Alas, one-other-gripping doesn't seem to generalise to more than three concerns. The players are skilled but also uniquely themselves. The world cup wouldn't be nearly as interesting if it were just unthinking robots fighting to see who can run around and kick things the best My drip coffee maker makes me coffee that I find more enjoyable than stuff from a barista at a fraction of the price. I don't know why you would assume privilege from a comment like that. If anything, it's the unprivileged kids who would know that you'd get your hand slapped for folding clothes like that. Speaking as a relatively unprivileged kid from Southeast Asia, my parents were so busy working they just didn't have time and energy to care about me, let alone how well i fold my laundry.But that's just one data point. When AI is a the forefront of scientific breakthroughs and all human endeavor is painfully minuscule compared to AI, we'll still be calling it slop as we all hypocritically generate more and more slop. God this whole comment section is weird.It's like all the people who are so proud they don't use dishwashers. Nobody is proud of this. Designing a non humanoid robot is the way to go. It’s not demonstrated yet as we still have to see a robot (humanoid or not) doing mundane tasks in full autonomy, efficiently and convincingly.It still looks inefficient to have a humanoid robot vacuum a house by using an ergonomic tool designed around the human body. I can't believe someone shares my point of view. We have already many non humanoid robots to do boring stuff (underwater maintenance, stuff like that). For some reasons people tend to think that for robots to do stuff close to humans, we need human like robots.We do not. I want a swarm of insects-like robots to fight mosquitos. Just a few arms that does the job neatly is more than enough. This is amazing! I can see in a few years having Home Assistant use an LLM to coordinate my home while I'm away. Tell the LLM to make an inventory of my home and then send the vacuum and the robot around to clean everything up. A multimodal LLM can inspect pictures for items that need to go in the dishwasher, the laundry or needs polishing and reorganise my closets to best allocate space, make the beds, take care of the plants. It has all day to do it while the solar panels provide ample energy anyway. Does this require a given hardware platform, or for example, could I run this model with a cheap mini robot with a single camera (i.e. good enough without depth), tracked treads, and an arm? I love this future. A robot that does all the chores relentlessly of the house while you spend time just working on the stuff you like. I think this robot is really well-made! Xiaomi seems to be quite controversial these days, but personally, I still really like the brand. I have Roborock (Xiaomi) automatic vacuum and it's fantastic technology. iRobot had a 10 year lead and did nothing with it. The robot is competent at avoiding clutter, clean the mops itself, leave my floor sparkling and so far has been extremely reliable. Got two Xiaomi phones in a row, one in 2018 for 250 euros (note 8 pro) and one in 2023 for 300 (13T) and I liked them a lot.My gripe with the second one is lowish battery life, it always bites me on longer trips when my gf's Pixel 8 lasts considerably more, but was also way more expensive.Will check battery reviews better next time I shop for a phone, I don't care much for the rest. I just can't deal with all the ads on Xiaomi phones. Switched over from Chinese phones, still remember my JiaYu G4 fondly, to Samsung few years ago where the situation is a bit more bearable As a house spouse I want this! Anyone have any recommendations for robot hardware where I could test this open-source model? It would be a cool/fun project to get it working on a little robot. Always remember this is the Model T from Ford, imagine what they will do in 10 or 100 years from nowExciting times we live in How are these models compared to world models by Waymo and Tesla, assuming their models are trained for recognising objects that can be further generalized for everyday chores? Compared how? Architecturally? In training methods? In performance?The remarkable thing about this modern "full VLA" approach is that it doesn't train for an explicit "world model" at all. There's no factored world state, no pool of trajectory rollouts being sampled from. Instead, everything is rolled into the same VLA stack that does all of it internally.The world model exists, but it's implicit - the VLA learns one, but only because it helps its training objectives. Trajectories are planned ahead sometimes, also implicitly, for the same reasons.It's a very "bitter lesson" way of doing things.As far as I know, Tesla's stack is somewhat similar architecturally - a highly integrated end to end model - but the training process for FSD does include factored "world modeling" side objectives to force the stack to develop driving-relevant internal world representations. So it's in a middle ground between a classic "world model" system and this flavor of VLA - albeit surprisingly close to the latter.Whether the world modeling side objectives are principled and justified or a leftover from the days of insufficient scale is unclear. This is very cool, and the paradigm that scales to bringing truly useful robots to virtually all domains of life - just as LLMs have scaled to that degree across virtually all domains of knowledge work.I picture a lot of perfectly tended gardens growing food. Please help me understand. Is it more effective to have a general model like this instead of a state machine that leverages other models and a deterministic set of code? That doesn't mean it'll be able to mop the floor better than a Roomba in a decade. No we haven't. Back in the 90's Honda's Asimov could barely walk. Now we have robots managing to run marathons. Of all the tasks I do, folding clothes takes a ridiculously small slice of my time. Is this and all the other robotics videos aimed at H&M staff? They're trying to show their investors a butler robot that can do all types of household chores.Folding fabric has traditionally been very hard to solve with robots; the ideal places to grab the fabric might be hidden, the fabric patterns are repetitive, and the material flops around, tangles in the grippers, and brings other pieces of laundry with it.Successfully sorting and folding a basket of laundry shows they've got a control system capable of performing some quite difficult tasks. They also use a large ring attached to the first zipper to help the robot grab it. This is not a realistic zipper, but I do appreciate them being honest and showing that grabbing small objects is still not perfect All you need to do to understand what task is more complex (for a robot to solve) is just to ask a 6-8 years old child to do it. It takes days before they "understand".So it cannot be that simple (for the reasons you mentioned). As a parent with 3 kids and both my wife and I working, folding clothes is the most tedious household chore. It's physically and mentally exhausting (unlike doing the dishes, which is only physically exhausting as I can wash piles of dishes while zoning out). And not to mention take so much time, a week's worth of unfolded pile of clothes for our entire household can take me 1-2 hours to fold uninterrupted (2-3 hours if I do it while watching Netflix or something). Small sharing tips, in case it can help: have the maximum of hangers possible. Doing household chores is good for the kids. I wouldn't wan't this robot driving around my household when I have small children anyway... Somebody will hopefully in advance, but probably only prompted by your call around the house mention a number of particular items will be needed later today, certainly before the second load will be dry. You are now searching for those items. Or arguing for why you really don't have time and parents are humans too and not unpaid servants.Another 10 minutes later the first load is washing. You set a timer, or perhaps have a machine that pings you when done. Of course, you have More Shit To Do, so you don't notice your alarm or ping and by the end of the morning when you hoped to sit down to enjoy maybe a whole 15 minutes of reading move the first load to the dryer and put the second load on. You discover that somebody never emptied the dryer. Finders keepers!1,5hr later you fortunately do not miss the alarm and take our the dry but not 100% dry laundry and move the second load to dry.3hr later you empty the second dryer load, because the run took longer because nobody ever fucking cleans the lint-container. At least you had time to fold (which to the many item-types in your household and your poor fine motor control certainly does not take you _just_ 15 minutes). A cat decides to kick over a pile and leave hair in a second pile, causing you to redo some work. 30 minutes in your are done and finally have time to fold the second driers worth.Do you get it now? Everybody's different, I guess, but for me this is absolutely a chore I would automate away, and I don't want to "force" another human being to do it for me. So is a pasta factory. Today it rolls and forms macaroni, tomorrow it makes bullets. Have you ever wondered why rigatoni are so wide? That's why. No, not really. The macaroni are formed by squeezing the dough through the openings, while the cartridge cases are made by stretching small metallic disks into cylindrical shape (and then stamping the bottom to make it flat and have a proper rim), so it's impossible to repurpose the pasta-forming machines into the casing-making ones.But it is an urban legend of sorts, along with "baby strollers with chain tracks instead of wheels", and shows how little most of us know about industrial manufacturing. Please keep in mind that "uncut footage of ..." is the same as those instagram videos where people throw a basketball backwards 20 meters while drinking beer, straight into the goal.It doesn't prove this is average performance. In fact, if it was, that's not what they'd be showing. From my skim of their paper, they have gotten robodojo 14% versus previous sota 9%. So its an improvement, but doesnt seem like a reliable model yet, which is fine. I also notice the model becomes worse with more data on the printer refilling task, so thats either a bad run or its a data point against the robustness. The transfer learning results are meaningful. 75% on new complex tasks from <10 hours of demo is really good. I believe this is a 2x improvement over the SOTA. 10 trials per task is standard for these papers but is pretty weak statistically. Memory tasks its weaker at but they have acknowledged it, and I cant see why this arch would have problems w that in the future. The fact it didn't do the 2 second tshirt fold trick shows it's just copying trained movements.This is Automation not AI. Well, couldn't it have been trained on the 2-second fold trick? Besides, it might require more precise or quick movements than a regular fold. More like pro-US exceptionalism, and any announcement for products created outside of it (whether it's EU or China) is met with doubts and skepticism.

Unless something is from Japan, then it's perfect and any criticism is because you don't appreciate its unique cultural heritage.

blocks.html_en: I've definitely noticed even more than that - any submissions painting the current USA in a good light are instantly and mercilessly buried (you can check my submissions for examples), while anything even remotely on the "USA bad" side gets to the top. It's really a sad state of affairs.

sentences.en: I've definitely noticed even more than that - any submissions painting the current USA in a good light are instantly and mercilessly buried (you can check my submissions for examples), while anything even remotely on the "USA bad" side gets to the top.
sentences.zh: 我肯定注意到的还不止这些——任何以良好的眼光描绘当前美国的提交内容都会立即被无情地埋葬（你可以查看我提交的例子），而任何甚至远程的“美国坏”方面的内容都会被放在首位。

sentences.en: It's really a sad state of affairs.
sentences.zh: 这实在是一种可悲的状况。

blocks.html_en:
You may take this as “USA bad”, comment, but let’s take the U.S. life expectancy reaches record high (in 2024) link you posted.

That sounds like a good thing and it is. But it’s not a US story. In fact, other wealthy countries didn’t just already have a higher baseline life expectancy pre-COVID, they rebounded to it post-COVID sooner than the U.S. was able to its already lower level.

So what this actually is, is a story of “global modern medicine good”, and unfortunately, even within that, the U.S. is among the worst performing.

sentences.en: You may take this as “USA bad”, comment, but let’s take the U.S. life expectancy reaches record high (in 2024) link you posted.That sounds like a good thing and it is.
sentences.zh: 您可能会认为这是“美国不好”，评论，但让我们以您发布的美国预期寿命达到历史新高（2024 年）链接为例。这听起来像是一件好事，而且确实如此。

sentences.en: But it’s not a US story.
sentences.zh: 但这不是美国的故事。

sentences.en: In fact, other wealthy countries didn’t just already have a higher baseline life expectancy pre-COVID, they rebounded to it post-COVID sooner than the U.S. was able to its already lower level.So what this actually is, is a story of “global modern medicine good”, and unfortunately, even within that, the U.S. is among the worst performing.
sentences.zh: 事实上，其他富裕国家不仅在新冠疫情之前就已经拥有更高的预期预期寿命基线，而且在新冠疫情之后，他们的预期寿命反弹得比美国还早。所以，这实际上是一个“全球现代医学良好”的故事，不幸的是，即使在这一点上，美国也是表现最差的国家之一。

blocks.html_en:
Is that so shocking on dominantly US forum? I wouldn't expect different situation, people are not 100% objective even if they try hard.

That being said, >95% of the world lives outside US and in recent times (culminating with orange man's second term but started way before) US has alienated literally every single other country, democratic or not. This is what happens when reins are firmly held by unstable people full of pettiness and mental issues, plus visibly corrupt too.

So division deepens as seen also here, and as much as 20th century was a century of US, 21st century is and will be one of China. Every move US makes now just pushes whole situation further into that direction. So folks cheer for their own camp, not some larger common group of aligned friends.

sentences.en: Is that so shocking on dominantly US forum?
sentences.zh: 在美国占主导地位的论坛上有这么令人震惊吗？

sentences.en: I wouldn't expect different situation, people are not 100% objective even if they try hard.That being said, >95% of the world lives outside US and in recent times (culminating with orange man's second term but started way before) US has alienated literally every single other country, democratic or not.
sentences.zh: 我不会期望不同的情况，即使人们很努力，也不是100%客观的。话虽这么说，世界上超过95%的人生活在美国境外，而且最近（以橙色人的第二个任期达到顶峰，但早在很久以前就开始了）美国实际上已经疏远了所有其他国家，无论是否民主。

sentences.en: This is what happens when reins are firmly held by unstable people full of pettiness and mental issues, plus visibly corrupt too.So division deepens as seen also here, and as much as 20th century was a century of US, 21st century is and will be one of China.
sentences.zh: 当权力被充满琐碎和精神问题的不稳定的人牢牢掌握时，就会发生这种情况，而且还明显腐败。因此，正如这里所见，分裂加深，就像20世纪是美国的世纪一样，21世纪现在是、也将是中国的世纪。

sentences.en: Every move US makes now just pushes whole situation further into that direction.
sentences.zh: 美国现在的一举一动只会将整个局势进一步推向这个方向。

sentences.en: So folks cheer for their own camp, not some larger common group of aligned friends.
sentences.zh: 因此，人们为自己的阵营欢呼，而不是为某个更大的、结盟的朋友共同群体欢呼。

sentences.zh: 话虽这么说，世界上超过 95% 的人生活在美国境外，而且最近（以橙色人的第二个任期达到顶峰，但早在很久之前就开始了），美国实际上已经疏远了所有其他国家，无论是否民主。

sentences.en: This is what happens when reins are firmly held by unstable people full of pettiness and mental issues, plus visibly corrupt too.
sentences.zh: 当权力被充满小气、精神问题、而且明显腐败的不稳定的人牢牢掌握时，就会发生这种情况。

blocks.html_en: So division deepens as seen also here, and as much as 20th century was a century of US, 21st century is and will be one of China. Every move US makes now just pushes whole situation further into that direction. So folks cheer for their own camp, not some larger common group of aligned friends.

sentences.en: So division deepens as seen also here, and as much as 20th century was a century of US, 21st century is and will be one of China.
sentences.zh: 因此，分裂加深，正如我们在这里看到的那样，正如20世纪是美国的世纪一样，21世纪现在是、并将是中国的世纪。

sentences.en: Every move US makes now just pushes whole situation further into that direction.
sentences.zh: 美国现在的一举一动只会将整个局势进一步推向这个方向。

sentences.en: So folks cheer for their own camp, not some larger common group of aligned friends.
sentences.zh: 因此，人们为自己的阵营欢呼，而不是为某个更大的、结盟的朋友共同群体欢呼。

blocks.html_en:
Pretty sure there isn't.

HN has lots of hellbanned bots lately (I have showdead on [1]) but I never notice ones pushing a political slant. But I suppose nation states conducting a campaign on Hacker News would be much too cunning to make the bots obvious

[1] because many posts get wrongly flagged, apparently in part by trigger-happy users who will flag anything with incidental similarities to AI prose (everyone, PLEASE STOP DOING THAT), in part because HN's automoderation is severe for new users, and in part because plenty of people get hellbanned either unfairly or although they do still write plenty of worthwhile posts. I even rescued someone who it turned out had been hellbanned by a mod by accident.

sentences.en: Pretty sure there isn't.HN has lots of hellbanned bots lately (I have showdead on [1]) but I never notice ones pushing a political slant.
sentences.zh: 肯定没有。HN 最近有很多被禁止的机器人（我已经在 [1] 上展示过），但我从来没有注意到那些推动政治倾向的机器人。

sentences.en: But I suppose nation states conducting a campaign on Hacker News would be much too cunning to make the bots obvious[1] because many posts get wrongly flagged, apparently in part by trigger-happy users who will flag anything with incidental similarities to AI prose (everyone, PLEASE STOP DOING THAT), in part because HN's automoderation is severe for new users, and in part because plenty of people get hellbanned either unfairly or although they do still write plenty of worthwhile posts.
sentences.zh: 但我认为民族国家在 Hacker News 上开展一场活动过于狡猾，无法让机器人变得明显[1]，因为许多帖子被错误标记，显然部分是由好战的用户标记的，他们会标记任何与人工智能散文偶然相似的内容（大家，请停止这样做），部分是因为 HN 的自动审核对新用户来说非常严格，部分是因为很多人要么不公平地被禁止，要么尽管他们仍然写了很多有价值的文章帖子。

sentences.en: I even rescued someone who it turned out had been hellbanned by a mod by accident.
sentences.zh: 我什至救了一个不小心被模组禁止的人。

blocks.html_en: Well. I already decided some time ago that I don't want to have my money go into US corporations as long as the orange King is living out his genocidal fantasies (and even without that, I am already annoyed to no ends about Apple, Microsoft, Facebook and Google abusing everyone else so blatantly and violently, including buying legislation - look at the mandatory age sniffing movement before you can access information on the www any longer; this is fascism, and children are abused as fake reason for change here). But I am not all that happy about China becoming so dominant in the whole product chain either. I think the production should be more balanced world-wide, globally. And also, very much so, locally. I am also aware that this is very hard to do, for various reasons, but still if I have a little influence then I really would rather not empower the USA or China any more than they already abuse everyone else here. And both do, by the way.

sentences.en: Well.
sentences.zh: 出色地。

sentences.en: I already decided some time ago that I don't want to have my money go into US corporations as long as the orange King is living out his genocidal fantasies (and even without that, I am already annoyed to no ends about Apple, Microsoft, Facebook and Google abusing everyone else so blatantly and violently, including buying legislation - look at the mandatory age sniffing movement before you can access information on the www any longer; this is fascism, and children are abused as fake reason for change here).
sentences.zh: 我不久前就已经决定，只要橙色之王还在实现他的种族灭绝幻想，我就不想让我的钱进入美国公司（即使没有这一点，我也已经对苹果、微软、Facebook和谷歌如此公然和暴力地虐待其他人感到恼火不已，包括购买立法——看看在你不能再访问万维网信息之前强制年龄嗅探运动；这是法西斯主义，儿童被虐待作为虚假的理由此处更改）。

sentences.en: But I am not all that happy about China becoming so dominant in the whole product chain either.
sentences.zh: 但我对中国在整个产品链中占据主导地位也不太高兴。

sentences.en: I think the production should be more balanced world-wide, globally.
sentences.zh: 我认为全球范围内的生产应该更加平衡。

sentences.en: And also, very much so, locally.
sentences.zh: 而且，在本地也非常如此。

sentences.en: I am also aware that this is very hard to do, for various reasons, but still if I have a little influence then I really would rather not empower the USA or China any more than they already abuse everyone else here.
sentences.zh: 我也知道，由于各种原因，这很难做到，但如果我有一点影响力，那么我真的宁愿不再授权美国或中国，也不愿他们已经虐待这里的其他人。

sentences.en: And both do, by the way.
sentences.zh: 顺便说一下，两者都是如此。

blocks.html_en:
I disagree with your assertion that Trump is somehow committing genocide against Palestinians while also acknowledging that the situation there is awful and I pray it ends soon.

But last I checked the Israeli-Palestinian conflict is a few thousand years older than our sitting president.

sentences.en: I disagree with your assertion that Trump is somehow committing genocide against Palestinians while also acknowledging that the situation there is awful and I pray it ends soon.But last I checked the Israeli-Palestinian conflict is a few thousand years older than our sitting president.
sentences.zh: 我不同意你的说法，即特朗普在某种程度上对巴勒斯坦人实施种族灭绝，同时也承认那里的局势很糟糕，我祈祷它尽快结束。但最后我查了一下，以色列-巴勒斯坦冲突比我们现任总统还要早几千年。

blocks.html_en:
western elites are currently at odds with the chinese elites and every impressionable idiot is being inundates with antichinese propaganda.

Lets be frank, every single issue we have with the CCP is either also being done by the west or in the works.

chinese oppression of the uighurs is nothing compared to whats going on in Gaza.

china forces prisoners to peel garlic while american prisoners are paid pennies a day to fight wildfires while being prohibited from being paid for those very same skills as firefighters when rehabilitated.

america is quickly workin on legislation to to make criticism of certain foreign governments illegal with very real reprucussons (revoking passports).

At least china has a working power grid and high speed train network.

sentences.en: western elites are currently at odds with the chinese elites and every impressionable idiot is being inundates with antichinese propaganda.Lets be frank, every single issue we have with the CCP is either also being done by the west or in the works.chinese oppression of the uighurs is nothing compared to whats going on in Gaza.china forces prisoners to peel garlic while american prisoners are paid pennies a day to fight wildfires while being prohibited from being paid for those very same skills as firefighters when rehabilitated.america is quickly workin on legislation to to make criticism of certain foreign governments illegal with very real reprucussons (revoking passports).At least china has a working power grid and high speed train network.
sentences.zh: 西方精英目前与中国精英不和，每个易受影响的白痴都被反华宣传所淹没。坦率地说，我们与中共之间的每一个问题要么也是西方造成的，要么是正在酝酿之中。中国对维吾尔人的压迫与加沙发生的事情相比根本算不了什么。中国强迫囚犯剥大蒜，而美国囚犯每天只得到几便士的报酬来扑灭野火，却被禁止获得与美国囚犯相同的技能的报酬消防员康复后。美国正在迅速制定立法，将对某些外国政府的批评定为非法，并予以非常真正的谴责（吊销护照）。至少中国有一个正常运行的电网和高速列车网络。

blocks.html_en:
It's possible to intelligently discuss the merits of Chinese society and also acknowledge that it's a brutal autocracy.

Making some kind of equivalence of the two in terms of the rights of the individual is so ridiculous that nobody is going to take anything you say after that seriously, you do your arguments a disservice by doing it.

sentences.en: It's possible to intelligently discuss the merits of Chinese society and also acknowledge that it's a brutal autocracy.Making some kind of equivalence of the two in terms of the rights of the individual is so ridiculous that nobody is going to take anything you say after that seriously, you do your arguments a disservice by doing it.
sentences.zh: 明智地讨论中国社会的优点，同时也承认这是一个残酷的专制制度是可能的。在个人权利方面将两者等同起来是非常荒谬的，以至于没有人会认真对待你之后所说的任何事情，你这样做是对你的论点造成伤害。

blocks.html_en: It's also relevant that China isn't open sourcing results because that's something China will do forever on some philosophical grounds. They do it because it is currently in the best interests of the Chinese state (and the world, as it turns out, coincidentally) but as soon as that stops being true there is zero chance it'll continue to be open source.

sentences.en: It's also relevant that China isn't open sourcing results because that's something China will do forever on some philosophical grounds.
sentences.zh: 同样重要的是，中国没有开源结果，因为这是中国在某些哲学基础上将永远做的事情。

sentences.en: They do it because it is currently in the best interests of the Chinese state (and the world, as it turns out, coincidentally) but as soon as that stops being true there is zero chance it'll continue to be open source.
sentences.zh: 他们这样做是因为它目前符合中国政府的最大利益（事实证明，巧合的是世界），但一旦这种情况不再成立，那么它继续开源的可能性就为零。

blocks.html_en:
Is there someone saying you should hate something?

If we want open source technology we should recognize that the current arrangement is non sustainable - the moment china doesn't have an american AI industry foil to play against it'll immediately close. It's up to the US to build more open source models. We already own the content used to train them, collectively. We should own the results as well. Make the big AI companies release weights.

sentences.en: Is there someone saying you should hate something?If we want open source technology we should recognize that the current arrangement is non sustainable - the moment china doesn't have an american AI industry foil to play against it'll immediately close.
sentences.zh: 有人说你应该讨厌某些东西吗？如果我们想要开源技术，我们应该认识到当前的安排是不可持续的——一旦中国没有美国人工智能产业的陪衬来对抗它，它就会立即关闭。

sentences.en: It's up to the US to build more open source models.
sentences.zh: 美国有责任建立更多的开源模型。

sentences.en: We already own the content used to train them, collectively.
sentences.zh: 我们已经共同拥有用于培训他们的内容。

sentences.en: We should own the results as well.
sentences.zh: 我们也应该拥有结果。

sentences.en: Make the big AI companies release weights.
sentences.zh: 让AI大公司释放权重。

blocks.html_en:
I keeping saying this, the best AI / LLM coming out isn't another question / answer chatbot or automate my email and essay.

It is Robotics! Finally doing my dishes and laundry for pete sake! And hopefully help me to find stuff I keep forgetting where I put it.

sentences.en: I keeping saying this, the best AI / LLM coming out isn't another question / answer chatbot or automate my email and essay.It is Robotics!
sentences.zh: 我一直这么说，最好的人工智能/法学硕士不是另一个问题/答案聊天机器人或自动化我的电子邮件和论文。它是机器人！

sentences.en: Finally doing my dishes and laundry for pete sake!
sentences.zh: 终于为了皮特而洗碗洗衣服了！

sentences.en: And hopefully help me to find stuff I keep forgetting where I put it.
sentences.zh: 希望能帮助我找到我一直忘记放在哪里的东西。

blocks.html_en: This super interesting link showing things that were impossible even a couple of years back, sitting below the day old news about Kagi's me too browser tells everything about HN audiences. West will fall behind in robotics and mostly because of the wrong priorities of its VCs and developers.

blocks.html_en:
> You're telling me we've finally got robots that can do our laundry?

No, we don't.

I wish we did, that would be amazing.

There have been demos like this over a decade. But they are just that, demos. Demos done in a controlled environment.

If there was a robot I could buy that could effectively fold laundry or do dishes, I would gladly drop 50-100k on it, but such a thing doesn't exist and likely won't for a long time.

sentences.en: > You're telling me we've finally got robots that can do our laundry?No, we don't.I wish we did, that would be amazing.There have been demos like this over a decade.
sentences.zh: > 你是在告诉我，我们终于有了可以洗衣服的机器人了吗？不，我们没有。我希望我们能做到，那就太棒了。十多年来一直有这样的演示。

sentences.en: But they are just that, demos.
sentences.zh: 但它们就是这样，演示。

sentences.en: Demos done in a controlled environment.If there was a robot I could buy that could effectively fold laundry or do dishes, I would gladly drop 50-100k on it, but such a thing doesn't exist and likely won't for a long time.
sentences.zh: 演示是在受控环境中完成的。如果我能买到一个可以有效叠衣服或洗碗的机器人，我很乐意在它上面投入 50-10 万美元，但这样的东西并不存在，而且很可能在很长一段时间内都不会存在。

blocks.html_en:
You would honestly pay 50-100k on a robot that just folds laundry or does your dishes? In the US you can get a maid that cleans once a week for $150-250 and would do a much better job. By the time that cost would equal this robot, the robot would be obsolete, not to mention any maintenance costs.

Also dishes are the easiest chore to do around the house, especially if you clean them after use instead of letting them pile up.

sentences.en: You would honestly pay 50-100k on a robot that just folds laundry or does your dishes?
sentences.zh: 老实说，你愿意花 50-10 万美元购买一个只折叠衣服或餐具的机器人吗？

sentences.en: In the US you can get a maid that cleans once a week for $150-250 and would do a much better job.
sentences.zh: 在美国，你可以花 150-250 美元请一位每周清洁一次的女佣，而且会做得更好。

sentences.en: By the time that cost would equal this robot, the robot would be obsolete, not to mention any maintenance costs.Also dishes are the easiest chore to do around the house, especially if you clean them after use instead of letting them pile up.
sentences.zh: 当成本等于这个机器人时，机器人就已经过时了，更不用说任何维护成本了。而且，洗碗是房子里最容易做的家务活，特别是如果你在使用后清理它们而不是让它们堆积起来。

blocks.html_en:
I honestly think I would. I have a maid come every two weeks which is of course not often enough for dishes/laundry. A maid that only came weekly couldn't stay on top of dishes either but they could do laundry fine sure. I do a lot of cooking and I find constantly doing dishes and loading/unloading the dishwasher to be a huge never-ending pain. A device that costs 100k and would free of me that sisyphean task forever would be a godsend. Also you can have the robot work anytime whereas a maid is probably on a schedule.

And not to move the goal posts, because my original post did only mention laundry and dishes, but I assume once a robot reached a point where it could do laundry/dishes effectively without supervision, that it could do a variety of other tedious household tasks. 50-100k would be well worth it IMO.

sentences.zh: 此外，你可以让机器人在女佣可能按计划行事的情况下随时工作。并且不要移动目标柱，因为我原来的帖子只提到洗衣和洗碗，但我认为一旦机器人达到可以在没有监督的情况下有效洗衣服/洗碗的地步，它就可以完成各种其他繁琐的家务活。

sentences.en: 50-100k would be well worth it IMO.
sentences.zh: 在我看来，50-10万是非常值得的。

blocks.html_en:
I honestly think I would. I have a maid come every two weeks which is of course not often enough for dishes/laundry. A maid that only came weekly couldn't stay on top of dishes either but they could do laundry fine sure. I do a lot of cooking and I find constantly doing dishes and loading/unloading the dishwasher to be a huge never-ending pain. A device that costs 100k and would free of me that sisyphean task forever would be a godsend. Also you can have the robot work anytime whereas a maid is probably on a schedule.

And not to move the goal posts, because my original post did only mention laundry and dishes, but I assume once a robot reached a point where it could do laundry/dishes effectively without supervision, that it could do a variety of other tedious household tasks. 50-100k would be well worth it IMO.

Wow, some people really dislike any inconvenience in your lives, don't you?

Wall-E was a documentary - just shot in the past.

blocks.html_en:
Technology has already removed a huge amount of inconveniences from our lives that we take for granted, such as not having to engage in backbreaking subsistence agriculture to survive. Why do you draw the line at a robot doing dishes but not a tractor plowing a field?

By your logic, why shouldn't I get rid of my dishwasher and laundry machine?

sentences.zh: 技术已经消除了我们生活中许多我们认为理所当然的不便，例如不必从事艰苦的自给农业来生存。

blocks.html_en:
Some things are necessary for survival and flourishing while others merely remove inconvenience from our lives.

Agricultural technology feeds the people we have around the globe (arguably we have too many people for the planet's capacity but that's a separate topic). Meanwhile some technologies just feed the hedonistic instinct.

Of course the line is blurry in places but cleaning up your messes is definitely the top end of the hedonism treadmill. I may be radical in my views here as I consider a personal automobile one such hedonistic luxury that the society would be better off without. Consider the Netherlands here. By just about any metric you care to measure it's one of the best flourishing societies in the world handily beating the US and Canada on an array of well being stats. Yet they are actively shunning cars and pushing their society towards the "inconvenience" of walking, cycling and public transport. Their society as a whole benefits in many ways.

Most of the world (with the USA leading the pack) is conveniencing itself into an early grave.

sentences.zh: 有些东西是生存和繁荣所必需的，而另一些东西只是消除了我们生活中的不便。农业技术养活了我们世界各地的人们（可以说我们的人口数量超出了地球的容量，但这是一个单独的话题）。

sentences.en: Meanwhile some technologies just feed the hedonistic instinct.Of course the line is blurry in places but cleaning up your messes is definitely the top end of the hedonism treadmill.
sentences.zh: 与此同时，一些技术只是满足了享乐主义的本能。当然，有些地方的界限是模糊的，但清理你的混乱绝对是享乐主义跑步机的最高端。

sentences.en: I may be radical in my views here as I consider a personal automobile one such hedonistic luxury that the society would be better off without.
sentences.zh: 我的观点可能有些激进，因为我认为私人汽车是一种享乐主义的奢侈品，没有汽车社会会变得更好。

sentences.zh: 然而，他们正在积极避开汽车，并推动社会走向步行、骑自行车和公共交通的“不便”。

sentences.en: Their society as a whole benefits in many ways.Most of the world (with the USA leading the pack) is conveniencing itself into an early grave.
sentences.zh: 他们的社会作为一个整体在很多方面受益。世界上大多数国家（以美国为首）都在为自己早日进入坟墓而努力。

blocks.html_en:
Thanks for taking the time to explain your point of view here.

I think what I'm struggling with is what criteria (or who) determines which inconveniences are acceptable to remove and which ones aren't. For example, why is a standard kitchen dishwasher acceptable but a robot that saves you 5-10 minutes a day of loading/unloading the dishwasher not? Is a regular Kenmore dishwasher acceptable, or does it push too far towards the top of the hedonism treadmill, and people should have to do dishes by hand?

> What will you do pray tell with that extra time? I doubt it will be spent on loftier pursuits. In fact I'm quite certain that for the vast majority it won't be.

I actually agree with you here. Judging by how most people spend their time, the majority will fill 10 minutes of toil saved with Youtube shorts and Instagram, but there are still a minority of people who will put that time normally spent on toil towards higher aims.

For the former category, if they were just doing mindless toil anyway, what does it really matter if they waste that time some other way?

sentences.en: Thanks for taking the time to explain your point of view here.I think what I'm struggling with is what criteria (or who) determines which inconveniences are acceptable to remove and which ones aren't.
sentences.zh: 感谢您花时间在这里解释您的观点。我认为我正在努力解决的是什么标准（或谁）决定哪些不便可以接受消除，哪些不可以接受。

sentences.en: For example, why is a standard kitchen dishwasher acceptable but a robot that saves you 5-10 minutes a day of loading/unloading the dishwasher not?
sentences.zh: 例如，为什么标准厨房洗碗机可以接受，但每天可以节省 5-10 分钟装载/卸载洗碗机的机器人却不能？

sentences.en: Is a regular Kenmore dishwasher acceptable, or does it push too far towards the top of the hedonism treadmill, and people should have to do dishes by hand?> What will you do pray tell with that extra time?
sentences.zh: 普通的肯莫尔洗碗机是否可以接受，或者它是否太接近享乐主义跑步机的顶端，人们应该用手洗碗？>请问你会用额外的时间做什么？

sentences.en: I doubt it will be spent on loftier pursuits.
sentences.zh: 我怀疑这笔钱是否会用于更崇高的追求。

sentences.en: In fact I'm quite certain that for the vast majority it won't be.I actually agree with you here.
sentences.zh: 事实上，我很确定对于绝大多数人来说不会。我实际上同意你的观点。

sentences.en: Judging by how most people spend their time, the majority will fill 10 minutes of toil saved with Youtube shorts and Instagram, but there are still a minority of people who will put that time normally spent on toil towards higher aims.For the former category, if they were just doing mindless toil anyway, what does it really matter if they waste that time some other way?
sentences.zh: 从大多数人如何度过时间来看，大多数人会用 Youtube 短片和 Instagram 来填补 10 分钟的辛苦，但仍有少数人会把平时花在辛苦上的时间用于更高的目标。对于前一类人来说，如果他们只是在做无意识的辛苦，那么他们以其他方式浪费时间又有什么关系呢？

blocks.html_en:
Thanks for taking the time to explain your point of view here.

I think what I'm struggling with is what criteria (or who) determines which inconveniences are acceptable to remove and which ones aren't. For example, why is a standard kitchen dishwasher acceptable but a robot that saves you 5-10 minutes a day of loading/unloading the dishwasher not? Is a regular Kenmore dishwasher acceptable, or does it push too far towards the top of the hedonism treadmill, and people should have to do dishes by hand?

> What will you do pray tell with that extra time? I doubt it will be spent on loftier pursuits. In fact I'm quite certain that for the vast majority it won't be.

I actually agree with you here. Judging by how most people spend their time, the majority will fill 10 minutes of toil saved with Youtube shorts and Instagram, but there are still a minority of people who will put that time normally spent on toil towards higher aims.

For the former category, if they were just doing mindless toil anyway, what does it really matter if they waste that time some other way?

blocks.html_en:
Because doing chores is not a complete waste of time. It's a mild physical activity and at the very least some rest for the brain and they eyes from the glowing screen.

As for who decides what is hedonism and what is a necessity? I think the society does - in a soft way. When people see others adopt more discipline and shun convenient doodads for slightly more physical work the society as a whole becomes a bit more badass.

I personally would rather bike and walk everywhere. Even if it takes longer and exerts more effort. If some day the majority sees it my way the society as a whole starts to soft push in that direction by altering the city infrastructure to be friendlier to bikes and pedestrians and less accommodating to cars. That's just one example.

Doing your dishes in the sink may feel like a terrible chore if you grew up with a dishwasher. I did not. I can live without one just fine. The benefit is that you get a bit of zen time away from the screen and there is no dishwasher to break and leak every few years.

sentences.en: Because doing chores is not a complete waste of time.
sentences.zh: 因为做家务并不完全是浪费时间。

sentences.en: It's a mild physical activity and at the very least some rest for the brain and they eyes from the glowing screen.As for who decides what is hedonism and what is a necessity?
sentences.zh: 这是一种温和的体力活动，至少让大脑和眼睛从发光的屏幕上得到一些休息。至于谁来决定什么是享乐主义，什么是必需品？

sentences.en: I think the society does - in a soft way.
sentences.zh: 我认为社会确实如此——以一种温和的方式。

sentences.en: When people see others adopt more discipline and shun convenient doodads for slightly more physical work the society as a whole becomes a bit more badass.I personally would rather bike and walk everywhere.
sentences.zh: 当人们看到其他人采取更多的纪律并避开方便的小玩意来进行稍微多一点的体力劳动时，整个社会就会变得更加糟糕。我个人宁愿骑自行车和步行去任何地方。

sentences.en: Even if it takes longer and exerts more effort.
sentences.zh: 即使需要更长的时间，付出更多的努力。

sentences.en: If some day the majority sees it my way the society as a whole starts to soft push in that direction by altering the city infrastructure to be friendlier to bikes and pedestrians and less accommodating to cars.
sentences.zh: 如果有一天，大多数人都以我的方式看待这一问题，那么整个社会就会开始朝着这个方向软性推动，改变城市基础设施，使之对自行车和行人更加友好，而对汽车则不太友好。

sentences.en: That's just one example.Doing your dishes in the sink may feel like a terrible chore if you grew up with a dishwasher.
sentences.zh: 这只是一个例子。如果您从小就用洗碗机长大，那么在水槽里洗碗可能会感觉像是一件可怕的苦差事。

sentences.en: I did not.
sentences.zh: 我没有。

sentences.en: I can live without one just fine.
sentences.zh: 没有人我也能活得很好。

sentences.en: The benefit is that you get a bit of zen time away from the screen and there is no dishwasher to break and leak every few years.
sentences.zh: 这样做的好处是，你可以远离屏幕，享受一些禅宗时光，而且洗碗机每隔几年就不会破裂和漏水。

blocks.html_en:
The pessimism in the comments here is entirely the reflection of the times we're living in.

Technology got weaponized against the average citizen.

It has become a synonym for unemployment, enshittification, mass surveillance and social erosion.

If this video appeared in 60's or 70's, people would be in awe imagining the future where robots do everything.

sentences.en: The pessimism in the comments here is entirely the reflection of the times we're living in.Technology got weaponized against the average citizen.It has become a synonym for unemployment, enshittification, mass surveillance and social erosion.If this video appeared in 60's or 70's, people would be in awe imagining the future where robots do everything.
sentences.zh: 这里评论中的悲观情绪完全是我们生活的时代的反映。技术被武器化了，针对的是普通公民。它已经成为失业、enshittiification、大规模监控和社会侵蚀的代名词。如果这个视频出现在60年代或70年代，人们会敬畏地想象机器人做所有事情的未来。

blocks.html_en: If this video appeared in 60's or 70's, people would be in awe imagining the future where robots do everything.

blocks.html_en: Yes but unemployment where a small cadre of rich elites own the robots and the rest of us are serfs is very different from the public owning and benefiting from the robots in the form of strong social services and UBI.

blocks.html_en:
This seems more likely than racism, or us exceptionalism as mentioned above.

People are and should be skeptical of this. The natural response would be

a) It will put me out of a job

b) I won't be able to afford it anyway.

sentences.zh: 自然的反应是a）这会让我失业b）无论如何我都买不起。
